1:
The chapter opens on a seam, and the grammatical-historical commentators are chiefly occupied with rejoining what the chapter-divisions have parted. Adam Clarke protests that this is scarcely another book at all — originally First and Second Samuel were one continuous history, "separated without reason or necessity" — and he reckons the reign that here begins to cover about forty years. Charles Ellicott ties the opening words directly to the close of the previous book: "after the death of Saul" looks back to 1 Samuel 31, and "when David was returned" to the Amalekite raid of 1 Samuel 30, so that the two books "really form one continuous narrative." He measures the ground as well: Ziklag, assigned in Joshua 19:5 to the far south and re-occupied after the exile (Nehemiah 11:28), lay a full four days' journey from Mount Gilboa, so the messenger must have left the field before David ever reached home. Keil and Delitzsch lift the eye to the whole book that here begins, laying out its four movements — David's reign at Hebron over Judah, his promotion over all Israel, the decline that followed his great sin, and the close of his reign — and observing that the history describes his kingdom chiefly "in its connection with the development of the kingdom of God."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the same setting for its providence and its portrait of David's temper. Settled again in Ziklag after recovering his people, David is already receiving "persons of quality" — the mighty men and captains who come to him day by day until he has "a great host, like the host of God" (1 Chronicles 12:22) — God "stirring up their hearts," for "the secret springs of revolutions are unaccountable, and must be resolved into that Providence which turns all hearts as the rivers of water." Henry marks especially that David had posted no spies about the camp to bring him early news, "a sign that he desired not Saul's woeful day, nor was impatient to come to the throne": "He that believes does not make haste, takes good news when it comes and is not uneasy while it is in the coming."

The smaller notes fill in the scene. The reformed John Gill fixes the moment as the third day after the battle, and the wesleyan John Wesley observes that Ziklag, though the Amalekites had burned it, was not so consumed that David and his men could not lodge there. The witnesses agree in what they make of this opening: it is a hinge between two reigns, dated and mapped with care, on which a patient man waits for a crown he will not snatch.
2:
A messenger arrives on the third day, his clothes rent and earth upon his head, and falls before David — and the witnesses at once divide over what the gesture means and whether the man can be believed. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott insists that this "obeisance" was "not merely an act of Oriental respect, but was intended as a recognition of David's rank as having now become king"; the torn garments and the dust, though he was himself an Amalekite (as the sequel shows), were the customary marks of sorrow for the defeat of David's people and the death of their king. Keil and Delitzsch keep close to the running dialogue — David asks "From whence comest thou?" and the man answers, "Out of the camp of Israel am I escaped" — and take his claim at face value as far as the general facts go.

It is the wesleyan Adam Clarke who opens the great question that will run through the whole chapter. "The whole account which this young man gives is a fabrication," he declares; "in many of the particulars it is grossly self-contradictory." For Clarke there is only one fact in the story: the bringing of the crown and bracelets, "which, as he appears to have been a plunderer of the slain, he found on the field of battle." The tale of despatching Saul he invented "merely to ingratiate himself with David." Thus from his first appearance the messenger is, on Clarke's reading, a scavenger dressed as a mourner, trading on a lie he expects to be rewarded.

The reformed John Gill adds only that the third day here is counted from the battle in which Saul fell. Yet the small notice repays attention, for it sets up the irony the chapter will exploit. The man has arranged everything — the rent robe, the dust, the low bow — to present himself as the first to do homage to the new king; and, as Henry will say at v.16, he proves instead to be the first to receive from David a sentence of death. The witnesses do not finally agree on whether he was ever truly in the ranks of Israel or merely haunted the edges of the rout to strip the fallen; but they agree that his posture of grief is a calculation, and that David, who could read men, will not be flattered by it.
3:
David's blunt "From whence comest thou?" and the man's answer, "Out of the camp of Israel am I escaped," draw the witnesses into a small but telling question: was this Amalekite really a soldier of Saul's army, or a stranger who happened upon the wreck of it? The reformed John Gill notices the suspicion already stirring in David: "It is very likely by his appearance and circumstances he suspected from whence he came." The torn clothing and the dust that were meant to win sympathy have instead set the king on his guard, so that the interrogation begins not with welcome but with probing.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the man's claim against his own later words. It has been questioned, he grants, whether the Amalekite "had actually been in the army of Israel," and the phrase "as I happened by chance upon Mount Gilboa" in v.6 has been cited to make his presence merely accidental. But Ellicott answers that "no one who is not concerned in the matter is likely to stray into the midst of a battle," and prefers to refer the "by chance" not to his being on the field at all but to his stumbling upon the wounded Saul. Here, at least, the man "certainly claims to have been a part of the camp of Israel," and — whatever the truth of his later boast — the general facts he now reports, the defeat and the death of Saul and Jonathan, are true, "as they really occurred."

So the witnesses draw the line that governs the whole interview. The framework of the man's report is sound: the army of Israel has broken, the king and his son have fallen, and he has escaped with his life from the disaster. It is the particulars — how Saul died, and by whose hand — that will prove doubtful. Ellicott's careful separation of the credible outline from the incredible detail is exactly the caution David himself shows: he accepts nothing on the man's mere say-so, but presses him step by step, "How went the matter?" and "How knowest thou?", refusing to let a self-serving witness settle either the fact of Saul's death or the manner of it until the evidence compels him. The plain lesson the commentators draw is one of discernment — that a report may be true in its substance and false in its self-flattering additions, and that the wise hearer, like David, distinguishes the two.
4:
"How went the matter? I pray thee, tell me." David's question, and the crushing threefold answer — the people fled, many fell, and Saul and Jonathan are dead — draw from the witnesses a study in how bad news is broken and how it is heard. The reformed John Gill glosses the king's demand simply: he asks "how went the battle? on which side the victory?" — the anxious inquiry of one whose whole future hangs on the reply. Keil and Delitzsch attend to the grammar of the messenger's response, noting how the little word "namely" introduces the mounting catalogue of loss: "not only have many of the people fallen, but Saul and Jonathan his son are also dead." The disaster is reported in a rising scale — rout, slaughter, and at last the death of the king and the prince.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the psychology of it. David, he says, "was very desirous to know how the matter went, as one that had more reason than any to be concerned for the public"; and the messenger "told him very distinctly that the army of Israel was routed, many slain, and, among the rest, Saul and Jonathan." Henry fastens on the messenger's selectiveness: "He named only Saul and Jonathan, because he knew David would be most solicitous to know their fate; for Saul was the man whom he most feared and Jonathan the man whom he most loved." In that single sentence Henry catches the whole tragedy of David's divided heart — the same battle has swept away at once the enemy who hunted him and the friend who loved him as his own soul, so that the news can bring him neither clean relief nor simple sorrow but only a grief tangled with dread.

The witnesses do not dispute one another here; they agree that this verse is the hinge of the narrative, the moment the report of the field first reaches the future king. Yet they also mark, with Henry, the messenger's calculation even in his grief-telling: he passes lightly over Jonathan and dwells on Saul, "thinking that he should be welcome for that, and rewarded as one that brought good tidings." He measures David by himself, assuming that the fall of an enemy must be welcome; and the whole chapter turns on how wrong that assumption proves. The plain report of v.4 — Israel broken, its king and its noblest son dead upon Gilboa — is the ground on which David's mourning, and his justice, will be built.
5:
David will not act on rumour, and his second question — "How knowest thou that Saul and Jonathan his son be dead?" — turns the witnesses to the theme of evidence. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read it as the demand that opens the man's whole set-piece narrative (vv.6–10): to David's pressing "how knowest thou this," the young man replies, "I happened to come up to the mountains of Gilboa, and saw Saul leaning upon his spear," and then unfolds the tale of the chariots pressing hard, the king's turning, the request to be slain, and the crown and bracelet carried off. The question thus functions as the hinge between the bare report and the circumstantial story the Amalekite is so eager to tell.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the question a window into David's character. He was desirous to know "the certainty of the report concerning Saul and Jonathan," Henry says, "either because he was not forward to believe it or because he would not proceed upon it to make his own claims till he was fully assured of it." Both motives honour David. He is slow to believe the death of the Lord's anointed, not snatching at the news as a man snatches at good fortune; and even were it true, he will not begin to press his own title to the throne until the fact is beyond doubt. Where the messenger expects eagerness, he meets caution; where he looks for a claimant impatient for a crown, he finds a man who waits.

The reformed John Gill adds only that David addressed "the young man that told him these tidings," but the framing matters. The witnesses agree that this short verse is not idle curiosity but judicial care: David is examining a witness. He has already received the general fact of the defeat from many quarters, as the crowds resorting to Ziklag showed; what he now requires is proof of the death of Saul in particular, and the manner of it. The irony the commentators feel here is that the Amalekite, invited to establish his credit, will in the telling establish only his guilt — for the very story he offers as proof that David should reward him becomes, out of his own mouth, the confession on which David will condemn him. The demand "How knowest thou?" is the door through which the man walks to his own sentence.
6:
The Amalekite begins his account — "As I happened by chance upon mount Gilboa, behold, Saul leaned upon his spear; and, lo, the chariots and horsemen followed hard after him" — and the grammatical-historical commentators test it against the terrain and against the true record of Saul's death. Charles Ellicott reconstructs the movement of the battle: it "appears to have been joined in the plain of Jezreel, but when the Israelites were routed they naturally fled up the mountain range of Gilboa, though apparently much scattered," and it was in this straggling flight that the man "happened upon that part of the mountain where Saul was." Yet Ellicott sets the story beside the plain narrative of 1 Samuel 31:3–6 and finds it strained: "it is uncertain whether the man saw Saul at all before his death, and it is extremely unlikely that he found him without warriors or armour-bearer, wounded and alone." Keil and Delitzsch concede that the substance of the report "was placed beyond all doubt by the king's jewels that were brought" — the crown and armlet are hard evidence that the man had at least reached the body — even while the details remain suspect.

The reformed John Gill turns aside a rabbinic guess about the messenger's identity. Some had supposed him to be Doeg the Edomite, or Doeg's son, the old enemy of David and butcher of the priests; but Gill will have none of it: "there is no reason to believe he was either of them, who cannot be thought to be well disposed to David." The man is simply what he says, a wandering Amalekite, not a figure smuggled in from the earlier history.

So the witnesses hold together the two things this verse forces upon them: the report is anchored to a real place and a real disaster, yet its picture of a solitary, abandoned Saul propped on his spear does not square with the account already given in 1 Samuel 31, where the king dies by his own hand with his armour-bearer beside him. The commentators are not yet ready to pronounce the whole a fabrication — the jewels forbid dismissing him out of hand — but they are already marking the seams where the Amalekite's self-serving story pulls away from the truth, preparing the reader for the verdict that David's justice will shortly deliver.
7:
"And when he looked behind him, he saw me, and called unto me. And I answered, Here am I." The witnesses treat this quiet turn in the story as one more brush-stroke in a portrait the Amalekite is painting for effect, and they read it with the same wary eye they have brought to the whole speech. The reformed John Gill supplies the plain sense of the king's backward glance: Saul "looked behind him" to see "how near the enemy was, and who were pursuing him" — the instinctive movement of a hunted man measuring the distance between himself and the chariots that press hard upon him.

Yet the very vividness of the detail is what the grammatical-historical reading finds telling. The Amalekite composes his scene with a novelist's care: the king turning, catching sight of a lone stranger amid the rout, calling out to him, and the ready answer, "Here am I." Every stroke is calculated to place the speaker at Saul's side in the king's last extremity, an intimate witness and, he will claim, an obedient servant of the royal will. But the fuller record of Saul's end in 1 Samuel 31, where the wounded king turns to his own armour-bearer and, refused, falls upon his sword, leaves no room for this convenient stranger summoned out of nowhere. The improbabilities Ellicott pressed at v.6 — that Saul should be found alone, without warriors or armour-bearer — press again here: who is this man that the dying king should turn to him, and where are those who fought and fell around their sovereign?

The witnesses, then, take the verse as a specimen of the whole. It is smoothly told and internally plausible, and that is precisely its danger; a lie is most persuasive when its particulars are circumstantial. David, examining the witness, lets him run on — "he saw me, and called unto me" — because the more the man elaborates, the more he commits himself, and the fuller the confession will be on which he is finally judged. The stranger who answers "Here am I" imagines he is stepping forward into royal favour; he is in fact stepping forward to be identified, questioned as to his nation, and condemned. The commentators find in this small exchange the pattern of the chapter: a self-serving story, told to win a reward, that furnishes instead the evidence for a sentence.
8:
"Who art thou? ... I am an Amalekite." On this exchange the witnesses converge, for the man's nation is the hinge of the whole story. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott rehearses the long feud that the single word "Amalekite" awakens: this people were "hereditary foes of Israel," having assaulted them on their coming out of Egypt (Exodus 17:8–13) and again in the wilderness (Numbers 14:45; Deuteronomy 25:18), and repeatedly joining Israel's enemies in the days of the judges (Judges 3:13; Judges 6:3). Years before, Saul had shattered them (1 Samuel 15); Ellicott suggests the present messenger may either "have attached himself to the army of the conqueror, or have been compelled, according to ancient custom, to serve in its ranks" — and he notes the sharp irony that another Amalekite band had, just days before, felt the edge of David's own sword, though of that blow this man could have known nothing. The reformed John Gill gives the human reason for Saul's question: not seeing who stood behind him, the king "was willing to know whether a friend or an enemy."

The wesleyan Adam Clarke presses the deepest irony of all, borrowing a remark of Dr. Delaney: it was "an Amalekite" who "took that crown from off the head of Saul, which he had forfeited by his disobedience in the case of Amalek." The circle closes with terrible neatness. Saul lost his kingdom because he spared Amalek against the command of God (1 Samuel 15:9); and now it is an Amalekite who strips the royal diadem from his corpse and carries it to his supplanter. The nation Saul would not destroy furnishes the hand that plunders his crown.

So the witnesses make this brief question and answer bear the moral weight of the chapter. The confession "I am an Amalekite" both explains the man's presence — a foreign scavenger, or reluctant conscript, drifting through the wreck of Israel's army — and seals his doom, for it is precisely as an Amalekite, of the devoted nation, that David will hold his life forfeit. Where the messenger names his people to lend colour to his tale, the commentators hear the note of a righteous providence: the enemy Saul refused to smite has become the instrument of God's justice upon Saul's house, and will himself fall under the justice of the king he came to flatter.
9:
"Stand, I pray thee, upon me, and slay me: for anguish is come upon me, because my life is yet whole in me." The witnesses divide between doubting that Saul ever spoke these words and drawing from them, if he did, a profound lesson on the misery of a guilty end. The reformed John Gill voices the first suspicion plainly: "it can hardly be thought Saul would say" this, "since he might as well have died by the hands of the uncircumcised Philistines, which he endeavoured to avoid, as by the hands of an Amalekite." The request rings false in Gill's ear, one more crack in the messenger's story. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers on the strange word rendered "anguish," which "occurs only here": the Rabbis explain it of cramp, others of giddiness, and the ancient versions cannot agree; probably it names "some effect of his wound which incapacitated him for further combat" rather than either sense our version prints.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, taking the words as they stand, turns them into a meditation on Saul's wretchedness. Here is a king "sick of his dignity and willing to be trampled upon, sick of his life and willing to be slain," so that Henry cries, "Who then would be inordinately fond of life or honour?" He hears in the plea the terror of a soul overtaken by its own past: "If his conscience now brought to mind the javelin he had cast at David, his pride, malice, and perfidiousness, and especially the murder of the priests, no marvel that anguish came upon him." His great sentence is the theology of the whole scene: "Sense of unpardoned guilt will make death indeed the king of terrors. Those that have baffled their convictions will perhaps, in their dying moments, be overpowered by them." Henry even weighs the marginal reading that Saul's own armour or embroidered coat hindered the fatal stroke, and draws the homely warning, "Let no man's clothes be his pride, for it may so happen that they may be his burden and snare." He recalls, too, the dreadful state of those who "desire to die, and death flee from them" (Revelation 9:6).

Between these readings the witnesses hold a real tension: Gill and Ellicott suspect the speech is invention, or at least garbled, while Henry mines it for its truth about a man dying without hope. Yet they meet in the underlying judgement — that whether or not Saul begged this Amalekite to end him, the picture of a king weary of a life he cannot bear and cannot escape is a fearful emblem of what it is to come to the last hour with an unquiet conscience.
10:
"So I stood upon him, and slew him ... and I took the crown that was upon his head, and the bracelet that was on his arm, and have brought them hither unto my lord." Here the Amalekite reaches the point of his whole errand, and the witnesses weigh both his claim to have killed the king and the regalia he lays at David's feet. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott pronounces the boast false: "this story is inconsistent with that given in 1 Samuel 31:4–5, and was evidently invented by the Amalekite to gain favour with David." Yet he grants the cunning of it — the man "is careful not to carry the story too far," claiming only that Saul was slain "at his own request, and after being mortally wounded" — and he concedes the force of the crown and bracelet as evidence: the messenger "must have been one of the first to find the body of Saul after his death," and his offering of "these emblems of royalty shows that the Amalekite recognised David as the future king."

The wesleyan Adam Clarke examines the regalia itself. The "crown" was "probably no more than a royal fillet or diadem," and the bracelet a like ensign of sovereignty; and he recalls the eastern custom whereby a prince bestows crown and bracelets "when investing others with dominion," asking whether Saul wore these "in token of his being God's vicegerent." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the same ornaments as a parable of pride and providence. Saul was "foolishly fond of these" trinkets, wearing them into the field and so making himself "a fair mark for the archers"; "as pride feels no cold, so it fears no danger." Henry dismisses the Jewish tradition that the Amalekite was Doeg's son as "a groundless conceit," and lifts the whole to its providential height: "David had been long waiting for the crown, and now it was brought to him by an Amalekite. See how God can serve his own purposes of kindness to his people, even by designing (ill-designing) men, who aim at nothing but to set up themselves." The reformed John Gill supplies only the grim mechanics — the man "pressed with all his weight upon his body, that so the spear might pierce through him."

The witnesses thus agree on the verdict that matters. The killing is almost certainly a lie, told to earn a reward; but the crown is real, and its journey from Saul's head to David's hand is the work of a God who bends even a liar's greed to fulfil his promise. The messenger meant to buy favour; he delivered instead both the evidence of his own guilt and the token of David's kingdom.
11:
"Then David took hold on his clothes, and rent them; and likewise all the men that were with him." Against every expectation of the messenger, David breaks not into triumph but into mourning, and the witnesses gather almost wholly around the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who reads the torn robe as the measure of the man's heart. "So far was he from falling into a transport of joy, as the Amalekite expected, that he fell into a passion of weeping," rending his clothes and, in the verse following, mourning and fasting — "not only for his people Israel and Jonathan his friend but for Saul his enemy." Henry finds in this the mark of both honour and grace: David acted "as a man of honour," refusing to "insult over those that are fallen," and "as a good man and a man of conscience, that had forgiven the injuries Saul had done him and bore him no malice."

Henry presses the point into David's own psalms. Knowing well the warnings that "if we rejoice when our enemy falls the Lord sees it, and it displeases him" (Proverbs 24:17–18), and that "he who is glad at calamities shall not go unpunished" (Proverbs 17:5), David proves here that those passages in his songs which "express his desire of, and triumph in, the ruin of his enemies, proceeded not from a spirit of revenge, nor any irregular passion, but from a holy zeal for the glory of God and the public good." The genuineness of the grief is Henry's chief concern: "His passion was so strong, on this occasion, that it moved those about him." The reformed John Gill adds the one clarifying touch of grammar — that David laid hold "not on the young man's" clothes "but his own" — lest the reader mistake the gesture for an assault on the messenger.

So the witnesses make this verse the turning of the whole chapter's mood. The rent garments are the outward sign of a tenderness the commentators find remarkable in a man so long and so cruelly hunted: David weeps for the king who threw the javelin at him, and his men, "at least in complaisance to him," weep with him. Henry sees here David's "natural temper," which "was very tender," and which "was kindly affected even to those that hated him." The pastoral point on which they rest is that true magnanimity does not gloat over a fallen foe; it can grieve for him, forgive him, and leave his faults buried with his body.
12:
"And they mourned, and wept, and fasted until even, for Saul, and for Jonathan his son, and for the people of the LORD, and for the house of Israel." The witnesses dwell on the breadth of this sorrow — its fourfold object — and on the wonder of who it is that mourns. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott insists on the reality of the grief: David and his men showed "the usual Oriental signs of sorrow by rending their clothes, weeping, and fasting," and though David thus heard at once "of the death of his persistent and mortal enemy, and of his own consequent accession to the throne, yet there is not the slightest reason to doubt the reality and earnestness of his mourning." He felt, Ellicott says, "a personal attachment to Saul, notwithstanding his long and unreasonable hostility"; and he adds the poignant observation that "the only deep mourning for Saul, with the exception of the men of Jabesh-gilead, came from the man whom he had hated and persecuted as long as he lived."

Ellicott then separates the strands of the lament. Besides his personal grief for Saul and for Jonathan, "David had both a religious and a patriotic ground for sorrow": the fallen were "parts of that Church of God which he so earnestly loved and served" — the people of the LORD — "and were also members of the commonwealth of Israel" — the house of Israel — "on whose behalf he ever laboured with patriotic devotion." He notes with disapproval that the Septuagint, "overlooking this distinction, has very unnecessarily changed 'people of the Lord' into 'people of Judah.'" Keil and Delitzsch keep the narrative moving, observing that David next asked the messenger for his descent and learned that he was "the son of an Amalekite stranger, i.e., of an Amalekite who had emigrated to Israel." The reformed John Gill vouches simply for the sincerity of it: they "inwardly mourned, and outwardly wept, no doubt sincerely."

The witnesses do not divide; they build one portrait of a grief that is at once personal, religious, and national. David weeps for a friend and for an enemy, for the church and for the nation, and his tears rebuke every narrow calculation of self-interest. The messenger had assumed that the death of Saul could only be good news to the man Saul had hunted; the fourfold mourning of this verse is the standing refutation of that assumption. What the commentators leave with the reader is the picture of a king whose sorrow is larger than his advantage — who fasts "until even" over the ruin of a people even as the crown that ruin brings him lies newly in his hand.
13:
"Whence art thou? ... I am the son of a stranger, an Amalekite." David returns to the messenger's origins, and the witnesses read the renewed question as the deliberate fixing of the ground on which the man will be judged. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the answer precisely: David "asked the bringer of the news for further information concerning his own descent, and received the reply that he was the son of an Amalekite stranger, i.e., of an Amalekite who had emigrated to Israel." The word for "stranger" is the term for a resident alien, a foreigner dwelling under Israel's law and protection — which makes the man's deed, on his own account, the more inexcusable, for he was no ignorant outsider but one who lived within the covenant nation and knew what it was to lift a hand against its anointed king.

The reformed John Gill canvasses the sense of the question more broadly. "From what place, or of what people and nation art thou?" is the plain reading; but Gill records the alternative of Abarbanel, who "thinks it neither respects place nor people, but that David thought he was another man's servant," so that the question means, "to what man did he belong?" Either way, David is establishing identity with a judge's precision, and the man, unwarily, confirms the very fact most fatal to himself.

The witnesses feel the dramatic irony that governs the verse. This is the second time the Amalekite has named his nation — he said it first to the dying Saul (as he claimed) at v.8, and now again to David — and each confession draws the noose tighter. He supposes he is still the honoured bearer of good tidings, elaborating his credentials before the new king; he does not perceive that David is assembling, out of the man's own volunteered words, the case for a capital sentence. That he is an Amalekite marks him as of the devoted nation; that he is a resident "son of a stranger" strips him of the excuse of ignorance; and that he twice boasts of slaying "the LORD's anointed" convicts him out of his own mouth. The commentators find here the quiet method of David's justice: he asks, he listens, and he lets the guilty man supply every particular of the indictment before he speaks the verdict of v.14.
14:
"How wast thou not afraid to stretch forth thine hand to destroy the LORD's anointed?" With this question David passes from mourner to judge, and the witnesses fasten on the phrase that carries the whole weight of the sentence — "the LORD's anointed." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that "it does not matter whether he fully believed his story or not, the man must be judged by his own account of himself." For David, "regicide was not ... merely a political crime"; he had shown "on more than one occasion of great temptation" — sparing Saul in the cave and in the camp (1 Samuel 24:6; 1 Samuel 26:9) — "that he considered taking the life of 'the Lord's anointed' as a religious offence of the greatest magnitude." It was, Ellicott adds, "an especially grievous thing for a foreigner and an Amalekite thus to smite him whom God had appointed as the monarch of Israel."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the juridical logic. David reproaches the man and then "commanded one of his attendants to slay him," passing sentence in the words, "Thy blood come upon thy head ... for thy mouth hath testified against thee" (compare Leviticus 20:9; Joshua 2:19). Keil quotes the searching gloss that this maxim cuts both ways: the man is condemned "out of his own mouth" (Luke 19:22), "whether thou hast done it or not. If thou hast done it, thou receivest the just reward of thy deeds. If thou hast not done it, then throw the blame upon thine own lying testimony." David, he notes, "could see plainly enough that the man was no murderer," yet would make of his example a warning that flatterers who traffic in blood shall have blood for their wages. The reformed John Gill adds that David understood the boast to mean more than pressing the spear home — that the man "drew his sword, and slew him."

The witnesses do not divide; together they expound the principle of the judgement. David's own long restraint toward Saul is the measure of his horror now: the man who twice refused, under bitter provocation, to touch the Lord's anointed cannot let stand the boast of a stranger who claims to have killed him for hire. The offence is at once against the crown, against God who set the crown on Saul's head, and against the plainest law of blood-guilt; and whether the boast be true or false, the boaster has, by his own tongue, made himself a self-confessed regicide.
15:
"And David called one of the young men, and said, Go near, and fall upon him. And he smote him that he died." The sentence is executed on the spot, and the witnesses — chiefly the grammatical-historical — are concerned to vindicate its lawfulness. Charles Ellicott brushes aside the modern scruple at the outset: "All question of David's authority to pronounce a capital sentence is here quite out of place. The Amalekite had just recognised him as king, and therefore acknowledged his authority." And even apart from that, Ellicott urges the plain circumstances of David's band: he "and his band of 600 outlaws were accustomed to live by the sword, and to defend themselves against Philistines, Amalekites, and other foes as best they could; and here stood before them one, by his own confession, guilty of high treason." The man has, in the same breath, hailed David as sovereign and confessed to slaying the previous sovereign; he cannot deny the jurisdiction he has himself invoked.

The reformed John Gill notes only that David summoned "one of the young men," his own servants that attended on him, to carry out the stroke — the king does not stain his own hand, but neither does he delay. Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward from the execution to the great elegy that follows, treating the swift justice as the necessary clearing of the ground before David can lift his lament over Saul and Jonathan.

The witnesses, then, take this verse as the resolution of the tension the whole scene has built. The messenger came expecting reward and receives a death-blow; the story he told to commend himself to the new king becomes the ground of his destruction. There is no cruelty in it for the commentators, but rather the coherence of David's character: the same man who wept and fasted for Saul in v.12 now vindicates the sanctity of Saul's person by refusing to let a self-confessed regicide profit by his crime. Justice and grief are of one piece. Having honoured the dead king with mourning, David honours him again with judgement, so that none may think the life of the Lord's anointed a cheap thing, to be taken and then paraded for a bribe. The command "Go near, and fall upon him" is the seal set upon the tears of the preceding verses.
16:
"Thy blood be upon thy head; for thy mouth hath testified against thee, saying, I have slain the LORD's anointed." David's parting word to the condemned man draws the witnesses to the juridical heart of the chapter: the Amalekite is judged out of his own mouth. The reformed John Gill reads the formula of blood-guilt straightforwardly — "the blood that he had shed, let him suffer for it; for as he had shed blood, his blood ought to be shed, according to the law of God" — and notes that for proof of the deed "he appeals to his own confession." The man needs no other witness against him than himself.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke states the matter with lawyerly economy: "If he killed Saul, as he said he did, then he deserved death; at that time it was not known to the contrary, and this man was executed on his own confession." Clarke thus grants what Ellicott and others suspected — that the boast may well have been false — yet holds the execution just, because a court proceeds upon the testimony a man gives of himself. This is the very principle Keil and Delitzsch drew out at the previous verse: the mouth that testifies "I have slain the LORD's anointed" convicts its owner "whether thou hast done it or not"; if the deed was done, he has his just reward, and if it was only vaunted, he must "be content with the wages of a wicked flatterer," for "according to thine own confession, thou art the murderer of a king, and that is quite enough to betray thine evil heart."

The witnesses stand united here, and the pastoral edge they leave is sharp. The Amalekite is a warning against the tongue that boasts of evil to curry favour. He measured David by himself, assuming that the fall of the Lord's anointed would be welcome news and the hand that hastened it worthy of reward; and by that miscalculation he "lied against his own head," as the older writers put it — his flattering, blood-stained boast recoiling upon him in a death-sentence. The commentators find in "thy mouth hath testified against thee" a maxim of enduring force: that men are righteously held to the account their own words give of them, and that to glory in a crime, whether committed or only claimed, is to pronounce sentence upon oneself. Saul, who spared the Amalekites against God's command, receives his death-wound (by the man's own tale) from an Amalekite; and the Amalekite, glorying in it, receives death from the king he sought to please.
17:
"And David lamented with this lamentation over Saul and over Jonathan his son." The witnesses turn from the executed messenger to the poet-king, and read the great elegy that follows as the truest measure of David's heart. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies the form: "lamented with this lamentation" is "the technical expression for a funeral dirge or elegy," such as David also composed over Abner (2 Samuel 3:33–34) and Jeremiah over Josiah (2 Chronicles 35:25). It is, he notes, "the only instance preserved to us (except the few lines on the death of Abner) of David's secular poetry," and he endorses Keil's estimate that it is "one of the finest odes of the Old Testament, full of lofty sentiment, and springing from deep and sanctified emotion, in which, without the slightest allusion to his own relation to the fallen king, David celebrates without envy the bravery and virtues of Saul and his son Jonathan, and bitterly laments their loss."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reflects on why grief should be set to verse at all. David "was a great master of his pen as well as of his sword," and by casting his sorrow into a poem he made it both "more moving and affecting" — so that "the passion of the poet is wonderfully communicated to the readers and hearers" — and "more lasting," fit to "continue long, from generation to generation." Above all Henry admires the charity of the elegy. David is "very generous to Saul, his sworn enemy": he "conceals his faults," for "charity teaches us to make the best we can of every body and to say nothing of those of whom we can say no good, especially when they are gone" — de mortuis nil nisi bonum — and he "celebrates that which was praiseworthy in him" without flattering him with virtues he did not have. The reformed John Gill notes simply that David "composed the following elegy ... and sung it in a tune agreeable to it."

The witnesses agree in what they find here: not a divinely inspired hymn — Henry marks that God is nowhere named in it, and that it was preserved not among the Psalms but in the book of Jasher — but a human masterpiece of sanctified feeling. David weeps for the enemy who hunted him and the friend who loved him in a single song, hides the one's sins and honours what was honourable, and so leaves the church a pattern for its own funeral speech: to bury a man's faults with his body, and to remember before God whatever in him was true and good.
18:
"(Also he bade them teach the children of Judah the use of the bow: behold, it is written in the book of Jasher.)" This parenthesis sets the witnesses their one sustained puzzle in the chapter — what "the bow" here means — and they carry both readings of it. The reformed John Gill takes it of the weapon: the words are "rightly put into a parenthesis, since they do not begin nor make any part of the elegiac song," and show that "amidst his sorrow ... he was not unmindful of the welfare of the people," commanding Judah to be "trained up in military exercises" and "the use of the bow," the more so because "the Philistines were expert in the use of it" and had done such execution with it in the late battle.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lays out the very same alternative, and cannot quite decide between its halves. On the one hand David may mean the weapon of war — the bow so central that "the sword and bow were put for all weapons of war" (Genesis 48:22) — willing his people to leave off the sling and recover the bow, since "it was the archers of the Philistines that bore so hard upon Saul" (1 Samuel 31:3). On the other hand "the bow" may be the title of the song itself, "Kesheth," so called "for the sake of Jonathan's bow, the achievements of which are here celebrated" — even as "Moses commanded Israel to learn his song" (Deuteronomy 31:19). The wesleyan Adam Clarke comes down firmly for the second: "the word kasheth is to be understood of the title of the song ... and not of the use of the bow," just as many Psalms bear such titles; it was named "The Bow" both "because it was occasioned by the Philistine archers" and because of "the bow of Jonathan, which returned not back from the blood of the slain."

On the closing clause the witnesses are agreed. The elegy "is written in the book of Jasher" — for Clarke "the book of the upright" of the Septuagint, for Henry "a collection of state-poems," the same volume from which the poetic fragment of Joshua 10:13 is drawn. Henry adds the shrewd reflection that "even songs would be forgotten and lost if they were not committed to writing, that best conservatory of knowledge." Keil and Delitzsch stand back to survey the structure the reader is about to meet: the ode falls into three strophes (vv.19–24, 25–26, and 27), each opening with the refrain "How are the mighty fallen!" So the debate over "the bow" — weapon or title — is real but friendly, and both sides serve the one aim: to see that David, even in grief, provides for his people and preserves their memory of the fallen.
19:
"The beauty of Israel is slain upon thy high places: how are the mighty fallen!" With this line the elegy proper begins, and the witnesses gather around its opening image and its haunting refrain. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes "the beauty of Israel" in the sense of "the glory or ornament of Israel, referring to Saul and Jonathan," and firmly rejects the alternative offered by the Syriac and some interpreters, who render it "the gazelle" as a poetic name for Jonathan. That reading is "uncalled for," Ellicott argues, "both because the words are spoken of Saul and Jonathan together, and because there is no evidence elsewhere that Jonathan was so called." The phrase "upon thy high places," he adds, echoes through the song (compare v.21; v.25), and this whole line "may be considered as the superscription of the whole song."

The reformed John Gill fills in the scene the line evokes: "the high mountains of Gilboa, where Saul their king, and Jonathan his son, a prince of the blood, and natural heir to the crown, and multitudes of young men, the flower of the nation, were wounded and slain." It is not two men only but a generation of Israel's best that lies fallen on those heights. The wesleyan John Wesley presses the freight of the words: "beauty" means "their flower and glory," Saul and Jonathan and their army; and he unfolds the cry "how" into three notes of astonished grief — "how strangely! how suddenly! how universally!"

The witnesses agree that this verse strikes the keynote of the entire lament. Twice more the refrain "how are the mighty fallen" will toll (at v.25 and v.27), dividing the ode into its strophes, as Keil observed; and each time it returns it gathers up afresh the wonder and the sorrow of a nation's glory brought low. What the commentators feel in common is the poet's refusal to begin with self-interest or with Saul's faults: he begins with Israel's loss, the "beauty" and "flower" of the people stretched dead upon the mountains, and lets the sheer strangeness of so great a fall — a king, a prince, an army — sound the note that every following line will deepen. The high places that should have been Israel's strength have become the theatre of her tragedy.
20:
"Tell it not in Gath, publish it not in the streets of Askelon; lest the daughters of the Philistines rejoice." The witnesses read this famous line as a cry of wounded love rather than a literal command, and they map the geography and custom behind it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that Gath and Askelon are "two chief cities of the Philistines, poetically put for the whole" — the one where David had himself once sheltered (1 Samuel 21:10; 1 Samuel 27:3–4), the other home to a famous temple where the Philistines would hang Saul's armour (1 Samuel 31:10). "Tell it not in Gath," he observes, "appears to have become a proverb" (compare Micah 1:10). Keil and Delitzsch explain the dread behind the wish: the tidings must not be carried "among the enemies of Israel, lest they should rejoice thereat," for "such rejoicing would only increase the pain of Israel"; and both he and Ellicott note the custom of women celebrating national victories "by singing and dancing" (Exodus 15:21; 1 Samuel 18:6) — the very "daughters" David dreads to hear exult.

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan John Wesley agree that the words are not a real prohibition. Gill: "this is not to be understood of a command of David, who could not hinder the victory ... being known at Gath," but "a wish it had not." Wesley says the same more fully — "this is not a precept, but a poetical wish; whereby he doth not so much desire, that this might not be done, which he knew to be impossible; as express his great sorrow, because it would be done, to the dishonour of God, and of his people." Wesley too explains the mention of "the daughters" by the custom of women celebrating victories "with triumphant songs and dances."

The witnesses converge on a single feeling. What grieves David is not merely that Israel has lost a battle but that the news will become a song in the mouths of "the daughters of the uncircumcised," a taunt against the covenant people and, through them, against their God. Ellicott remarks that the epithet "uncircumcised" is applied in the historical books almost solely to the Philistines, sharpening the reproach. So the line is heard by all as the reflex of a heart jealous for the LORD's honour: David can no more silence Philistia than he can raise the dead on Gilboa, but his impossible wish gives voice to the shame he feels that Israel's disaster should be turned into pagan festivity. It is sorrow speaking in the accents of poetry, not a general issuing an order.
21:
"Ye mountains of Gilboa, let there be no dew, neither let there be rain upon you, nor fields of offerings: for there the shield of the mighty is vilely cast away, the shield of Saul, as though he had not been anointed with oil." The witnesses take this the boldest stroke of the elegy and work out both its poetry and a genuine textual crux. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read it as a summons to nature to share the grief: "Even nature is to join in the mourning. May God withdraw His blessing from the mountains ... that they may not be moistened by the dew and rain of heaven, but, remaining in eternal barrenness, be memorials of the horrible occurrence." The "fields of offerings" he takes as "fields of first-fruit offerings," fruitful ground from which gifts were brought to God.

The crux is the closing phrase, and here the witnesses openly divide. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reports that "as though he had not been anointed" follows the Vulgate and yields the good sense "as though Saul had not been a king" — but he judges it "more than doubtful if the original can bear this construction," since "there is no pronoun in the Hebrew, and the word 'anointed' refers to the shield": Saul's shield, oiled for its preservation as metal shields were, was "thrown away uncared for." The wesleyan Adam Clarke goes further and emends the text, reading not beli ("not") but keley ("weapons"), so that the line runs "the shield of Saul, weapons anointed with oil"; he marshals a medieval manuscript, an early printed Bible, and the silence of the ancient versions in support, and prints the whole song in Hebrew and in Kennicott's Latin, urging that the warrior-word rendered "slain" throughout is better read "soldier."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry and the wesleyan John Wesley guard the curse from being taken literally. It is "a poetical strain," Henry says, "like that of Job, Let the day perish wherein I was born" (Job 3:3) — David does not truly wish any part of the land barren, but "to express his sorrow ... speaks with a seeming indignation at the place." Henry draws two lessons: that "the fruitfulness of the earth depends upon heaven," and that its fruits are "devoted to heaven," so that "to want that wherewith we should honour God" — the failed "fields of offerings" — "is worse than to want that wherewith we should sustain ourselves" (compare Joel 1:9). Wesley agrees it is "no proper imprecation, but a passionate representation of the horror" of the loss, and reads the disgrace of the "vilely cast away" shield as the shame of a soldier who has flung away his defence, Saul falling "as if he had been no more than a common soldier." Beneath the textual debate the witnesses are one: the verse is grief clothed in poetic malediction, and its sting is that the shield of the anointed king lay dishonoured on the field like any conscript's.
22:
"From the blood of the slain, from the fat of the mighty, the bow of Jonathan turned not back, and the sword of Saul returned not empty." Having cursed the place of the fall, the elegy turns to praise the valour of the fallen, and the witnesses expound its fierce battle-imagery. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch unfold the figure: "their bow and sword did not turn back until it was satisfied with the blood and fat of the slain," the poem resting on the ancient picture "that arrows drink the blood of the enemy, and a sword devours their flesh" (compare Deuteronomy 32:42; Isaiah 34:5–6; Jeremiah 46:10). He notes the deliberate division of the two great weapons between the two heroes, "so that the bow is assigned to" Jonathan "and the sword to" Saul — a poetic balance that will be sustained through the following verses.

The reformed John Gill reads the line as a tribute to unfailing prowess: Jonathan's bow "always did execution, the arrows shot from it pierced into men, shed their blood, and slew them; even they entered into the fat of the mighty," so that, like the arrows of other renowned archers, they "did not return in vain." The wesleyan John Wesley presses the same sense and draws out its pathos: the bow turned "not back" means "without effect," for their weapons "did seldom miss, and commonly pierced fat, and flesh, and blood, and reached even to the heart"; the sword "returned not empty" but came back "filled and glutted with blood." And Wesley finds the sting of the verse in the contrast it implies: "this their former successfulness is mentioned as an aggravation of their last infelicity." The very weapons that never once came home unbloodied have now, on Gilboa, failed their masters.

The witnesses do not divide; they build one picture of Saul and Jonathan as warriors whose bow and sword were, in life, invincible. The purpose of the praise is felt by all to be double. It magnifies the loss — such champions, whose arrows and blade never returned empty, are the men now lying dead — and it deepens the mystery of the defeat, that heroes so formidable should at last be overthrown. David, refusing to remember Saul by his sins, remembers him by his courage; and in setting the king's ever-devouring sword beside the prince's never-retreating bow, he binds the two men together in a single tribute of martial glory, preparing for the word of the next verse that in their lives they were lovely, and in their death not divided.
23:
"Saul and Jonathan were lovely and pleasant in their lives, and in their death they were not divided: they were swifter than eagles, they were stronger than lions." The witnesses linger on the charity of this praise, and on how far it can honestly be said of Saul. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read "in death as in life, the two heroes were not divided," and marvel that "notwithstanding their difference of character," and Saul's fierce hatred of the friend Jonathan loved as his own soul, "the noble Jonathan did not forsake his father." The gentle epithets, he grants, "apply chiefly to Jonathan," yet "they were also suitable to Saul in the earliest years of his reign," when his virtues "secured for him the lasting affection ... of the people"; and in his mourning "David forgets all the injury that Saul has inflicted upon him," celebrating "the more amiable aspects of his character." The images of eagle-swiftness (Habakkuk 1:8) and lion-strength crown the tribute. Charles Ellicott agrees the praise "applies peculiarly to Jonathan, but also in a good degree to Saul in his earlier years and his better moments, which David chose at this moment to recall," and truthfully "to them both in their relations to each other."

The wesleyan John Wesley is the most careful to justify the word "lovely" as applied to so troubled a king. Saul and Jonathan were "amiable, and obliging in their carriage ... both towards one another, and towards their people"; and as for Saul's savagery, Wesley excuses it as untypical: "Saul's fierce behaviour towards Jonathan ... was only a sudden passion, by which his ordinary temper was not to be measured," and his hostility to David sprang "from that jealousy and reason of state which usually engageth even well-natured princes." The concluding similes Wesley expounds plainly: they were "swifter than eagles," meaning "expeditious in pursuing their enemies," and "stronger than lions" in "their bodily strength, and the courage of their mind." The reformed John Gill keeps the accent on their concord, "no quarrel or difference with each other, only on the account of David," but agreeing "in the court, and in the camp, in their councils, and in their conduct."

The witnesses are united in the pastoral lesson they draw from this verse: David eulogizes only what was true. He does not praise Saul's piety, which he could not; he praises his courage, his early graciousness, and above all the love between father and son that even their tragic difference could not sever. The commentators find here a model for Christian eulogy — to recall a man's genuine excellencies without flattering him with virtues he lacked, and to let the grave bury the rest.
24:
"Ye daughters of Israel, weep over Saul, who clothed you in scarlet, with other delights, who put on ornaments of gold upon your apparel." The elegy summons the women of Israel to mourn, and the witnesses ask why they in particular are called, and what real benefit of Saul's reign the verse commemorates. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the "scarlet" and the "ornaments of gold" as the fruit of Saul's wars: the words refer "to Saul's division among the people of the spoil of his conquered foes, and to the prosperity resulting from his many successful campaigns." For, whatever his end, "notwithstanding that his light at last went out under the cloud of a crushing defeat, he had been on the whole a successful warrior." The Philistines, the Ammonites, the Amalekites and others "had felt the power of his arm," and under him "the relations of Israel to the surrounding nations had been wonderfully changed for the better."

The wesleyan John Wesley explains the address to the "daughters" by the same custom he noted at v.20: the women "used to make songs both of triumph, and of lamentation," and, being those "most delighted with the ornaments of the body," are fitly bidden to bewail the king who supplied them. As for the clothing itself, Wesley traces it to Saul's victories: "he procured them so much peace as gave them opportunity of enriching themselves," and "he took these things as spoils from the enemies, and clothed his own people with them." The reformed John Gill simply sets the women within the scene, weeping "in their mournful elegies," while Keil and Delitzsch carry the reader on toward the second strophe and its concentration upon Jonathan.

The witnesses do not divide; together they preserve the fairness of David's tribute. It would have been easy to remember Saul only by his cruelty and his ruin, but the elegy insists that his reign brought Israel genuine good — victory over encircling foes, peace, prosperity, even the finery of the women who now weep for him. David bids them mourn not a tyrant but a benefactor, and the commentators feel the generosity of it: the king who hunted David is here credited, before all Israel, with the wealth and security his campaigns had won. The verse is one more instance of the charity the whole ode embodies — to speak of the dead the truth that can be spoken to their honour, and to let a nation's daughters weep for what they had truly received at his hand.
25:
"How are the mighty fallen in the midst of the battle! O Jonathan, thou wast slain in thine high places." The refrain returns, and with it the elegy narrows from the two heroes to Jonathan alone, and the witnesses mark both the structure and the tenderness of the turn. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that this verse "is almost a verbal repetition of v.19," the opening cry sounding again to begin "the second strophe," which "only applies to the friendship of Jonathan." The recurrence of "how are the mighty fallen" is thus no mere ornament but the architecture of the ode, each return of the line opening a fresh movement of grief and drawing the mourning inward toward its most personal note.

The reformed John Gill hears in the repeated refrain the very pulse of David's sorrow: he laments "the mighty and valiant men of war, the common soldiers as well as their general officers," and "the repetition shows how much it affected him." The wesleyan John Wesley fastens on the phrase "in thine high places" and draws out its quiet pathos: they were the heights "which were in thy country, and (had not thy father disinherited thee by his sins) in thy dominions." In that parenthesis Wesley touches the whole tragedy of Jonathan — the true and generous heir who, but for Saul's disobedience, would have reigned over these very hills, and who instead lies slain upon them, his inheritance forfeited by his father's sin before ever it could be his.

The witnesses do not contend; they combine to show how the poem tightens its focus. The first strophe had gathered Saul and Jonathan together in a common praise of valour and love; now the refrain tolls a second time and the song bends toward the friend whom David loved above all. The "high places" that were the scene of the whole disaster at v.19 become here specifically Jonathan's — the ground of his lost kingdom and the place of his death — so that the public lament for a fallen army passes, by this hinge-verse, into the private grief of one man for another. What the commentators leave with the reader is the sense of a sorrow deepening as it narrows: the mightier the fall, the more it comes at last to a single beloved name.
26:
"I am distressed for thee, my brother Jonathan: very pleasant hast thou been unto me: thy love to me was wonderful, passing the love of women." On the emotional summit of the elegy the witnesses gather to expound the depth of a friendship the world has never forgotten. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the words: "distressed" denotes "the pinching or pressure of the heart consequent upon pain and mourning," and "wonderful" a love "to be wonderful or distinguished," while the "comparison to the love of woman is expressive of the deepest earnestness of devoted love." Charles Ellicott unfolds the same comparison: by likening "Jonathan's love for David to that of the faithful wife for her husband," David "shows his appreciation of that wonderful affection which had existed between" them "under the most untoward circumstances" — "such an affection as could only exist between noble natures and those united in the fear of God." These closing lines, Ellicott notes, "relate to Jonathan alone" and give voice to "David's own personal sorrow."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the wonder of it home. What made Jonathan's love "wonderful," he says, was that it was given by "a man to love one who he knew was to take the crown over his head, and to be so faithful to his rival: this far surpassed the highest degree of conjugal affection and constancy." From the loss Henry draws two truths of universal reach: "that nothing is more delightful in this world than a true friend, that is wise and good ... and is faithful to us in all our true interests"; and "that nothing is more distressful than the loss of such a friend; it is parting with a piece of one's self." He adds the sober note that "the more we love the more we grieve." The reformed John Gill catalogues the bonds that made Jonathan a "brother" — "by nation and religion," by affinity through David's marriage into Saul's house, and above all "by affection and friendship" — and observes that David's distress was "not on account of his spiritual and eternal state, which he doubted not was happy," but for the loss of his "pleasant conversation," his "counsel and advice, and assistance in his present circumstances."

The witnesses are wholly at one, and the pastoral point on which they rest is the sanctity of holy friendship. Jonathan's love, "passing the love of women," is set before the church as the pattern of a devotion that seeks nothing for itself — a prince loving the man who would supplant him, and loving him to the end. In lamenting it David honours not only his friend but the God in whose fear such love is possible; and the commentators leave the reader with the ache of the verse itself, that the dearest earthly ties are the very ones whose severing costs us most.
27:
"How are the mighty fallen, and the weapons of war perished!" A last time the refrain sounds, and the great ode closes upon it; the witnesses read this brief third strophe as the dying fall of David's grief. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe its office exactly: the verse "contains simply a brief aftertone of sorrow, in which the ode dies away." He fixes, too, the sense of its final image, which is not what it first appears: "'the weapons of war' are not the weapons; but the expression is a figurative one, referring to the heroes by whom war was carried on" (compare Isaiah 13:5), noting that Luther had already rendered it so, "die Streitbaren" — the men of war themselves. The mighty who have fallen and the "weapons" that have perished are one and the same: Saul and Jonathan and the flower of Israel, the living instruments through whom the nation waged its wars, now broken on Gilboa.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry hears in this closing refrain the summary of the whole. "The mighty have fallen" is, he observes, "three times lamented" across the song — at v.19, v.25, and here — "and so the strength of the people is weakened." Henry draws the civic lesson that "public losses are most laid to heart by men of public spirit," for David, though he "hoped God would make him instrumental to repair those losses," yet genuinely "laments them." The grief is not the calculation of a rival counting his advantage but the sorrow of a patriot over his nation's wound.

The witnesses agree in what they make of the ending. The refrain that opened the elegy at v.19 now closes it, framing the whole in wonder at so great a fall; and the poem, which began by summoning nature and the nations and the daughters of Israel to mourn, ends stripped to its barest cry — the mighty fallen, the men of war perished. The commentators leave the chapter here on a note of sanctified sorrow: David has honoured his enemy and wept for his friend, hidden the faults of the dead and praised their valour, done justice on the regicide and comfort to the mourning nation, and now lets his lament sink into silence with the same words it began, that Israel might never forget how her glory was slain upon the high places.
